reasoning: Following the cessation of the Ukraine transit route in January 2025 (which previously carried about 15 bcm/yr), Russia's pipeline capacity to the EU is limited strictly to the TurkStream pipeline, which has a nameplate capacity of ~15.75 bcm/yr (under 4 bcm per quarter). Russia's Western LNG production capacity (such as Yamal LNG and Portovaya) is approximately 25-28 bcm/yr. Even assuming 100% of this capacity was exported to the EU (which has never happened, as Asian markets receive a significant share), LNG imports would cap at roughly 7 bcm per quarter. Hence, the combined maximum physical limit of Russian gas delivery to the EU without the Ukraine transit or Nord Stream/Yamal-Europe pipelines is below 11 bcm per quarter. Prior to the Ukraine route closing, Russian gas imports in 2023 and 2024 averaged roughly 11-12 bcm per quarter. Stripping out the ~3-4 bcm per quarter from the Ukraine transit places the expected 2025 run rate securely in the 7-9 bcm range, far short of the 15 bcm threshold. Given these hard infrastructural limits and current market realities entering late 2025, there is virtually zero physical possibility of hitting 15,000 million cubic meters (15 bcm) in any single quarter.
